title: RGB votes 2.75% increase for 1-year leases, 5.25% for 2-year leases (2024)
content_summary: The RGB approved a 2.75% increase for 1-year lease renewals and 5.25% for 2-year renewals, effective October 1, 2024. The vote was 5-4, with tenant representatives dissenting. The board cited rising operating costs (insurance +12%, property taxes +4.5%) and the need to maintain building viability while acknowledging affordability pressures.

title: Mayor Adams announces City of Yes for Housing Opportunity final approval
content_summary: The City Council approved City of Yes for Housing Opportunity, the most significant citywide zoning reform in decades. The plan legalizes accessory dwelling units, allows modest density increases near transit, converts underused commercial space to housing, and removes parking mandates. DCP estimates the reform could yield 80,000+ new units over 15 years, though independent analyses suggest 40,000-60,000 is more realistic.

title: DOB permit filings for new residential buildings decline 18% in Q1 2025
content_summary: DOB monthly activity reports show new building permit applications for residential projects fell 18% in Q1 2025 compared to Q1 2024. The decline was steepest in Brooklyn (-24%) and Queens (-21%). Industry groups attribute the drop to expiration of 421-a, elevated interest rates, and rising construction costs. Manhattan held relatively steady due to luxury pipeline.

title: Federal Reserve holds rates steady at 4.25-4.50%, signals caution on cuts
content_summary: The FOMC held the federal funds rate at 4.25-4.50% at its March 2025 meeting, maintaining the level set in December 2024. The committee noted persistent core inflation above 2.5% and strong labor markets. Dot plot projections suggest 1-2 rate cuts possible in H2 2025. The 30-year mortgage rate remains near 6.9%, constraining housing affordability and new development financing.

title: HPD launches enhanced enforcement initiative targeting worst landlords
content_summary: HPD announced an expanded enforcement initiative targeting the 200 worst-condition buildings in the city. The program includes accelerated inspections, emergency repair deployments, and litigation against repeat-offender landlords. HPD Commissioner noted that heat and hot water complaints rose 8% in winter 2024-25, driven by deferred maintenance in outer-borough stabilized buildings.

title: HSTPA impact: Stabilized building sales volume drops 45% since 2019
content_summary: Analysis of NYC multifamily sales data shows that transaction volume for rent-stabilized buildings has fallen 45% since the passage of HSTPA in June 2019. Average price per unit for stabilized buildings dropped from $210,000 (2018) to $145,000 (2024). The report attributes the decline to elimination of vacancy decontrol, reduced IAI benefits, and tighter MCI rules, which collectively lowered expected NOI growth.

title: 421-a successor program stalls in Albany for third consecutive session
content_summary: Negotiations over a replacement for the 421-a tax abatement collapsed again in the 2025 legislative session. The Real Estate Board of New York (REBNY) and labor unions could not agree on wage requirements for construction workers on subsidized projects. Without a tax incentive, developers say most new rental construction outside Manhattan is financially infeasible. An estimated 15,000-20,000 units per year are not being started due to the program's absence.

title: NYC Comptroller report: Property tax revenue at risk from stabilized building devaluation
content_summary: The Comptroller's office published an analysis finding that aggregate assessed values of rent-stabilized multifamily buildings declined 12% since 2019 (inflation-adjusted), reducing the Class 2 property tax base by approximately $3.2B. The report warns that further rent freezes could accelerate this trend, potentially requiring tax rate increases on other property classes to maintain revenue neutrality.

title: Community Housing Improvement Program (CHIP) survey: 38% of stabilized landlords report negative cash flow
content_summary: An annual survey of rent-stabilized building owners by CHIP found that 38% reported negative operating cash flow in 2024, up from 28% in 2022. Median operating cost increases were 7.2% (driven by insurance +14%, labor +6%, utilities +5%) against average rent increases of 2.4%. Owners of pre-war walk-ups in the Bronx and northern Manhattan were most likely to report distress.

title: Census Bureau: NYC metro area net domestic out-migration slows to 52,000 in 2024
content_summary: Census population estimates show NYC metro net domestic out-migration of 52,000 in 2024, down from 127,000 in 2022 and 94,000 in 2023. International in-migration of approximately 180,000 more than offset domestic losses, resulting in net population growth. The slowdown in out-migration suggests the pandemic-era exodus has largely reversed, maintaining housing demand pressure.

title: Zara Mamdani wins NYC mayoral primary, pledges 4-year rent freeze
content_summary: City Councilmember Zara Mamdani won the Democratic mayoral primary on a platform centered on housing affordability, including a pledge to push for a 4-year rent freeze on stabilized apartments through RGB appointments. She also proposed a $5B public housing construction fund and expansion of good cause eviction protections. Real estate industry groups expressed alarm, warning of investment flight.
